coredata.dc:title: Latent fingerprints identification and ink-free printing of fluorescent lignin-derived CDs/PVA nanocomposite with multi-stimuli responsive shape memory behavior
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Wenhao
Zhou, Jie
Jiang, Mingyue
Sun, Qingfeng
Ye, Gaoyuan
Zhang, Xiaochun
Bi, Hongjie
coredata.dc:description: The development and comparison of latent fingerprint (LFP) are crucial for the detection and identification of LFP. However, research on stable, clear, and no background interference’s fluorescent materials for LFP recognition are scarce. In this study, lignin-derived carbon dots (CDs) with orange red emission were synthesized and combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix for identification of LFP and ink-free printing. The physicochemical and microstructure of the CDs and CDs/PVA nanocomposite have been precisely elucidated by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, SEM, and TGA to verify the reaction of CDs with PVA forming hydrogen bonds. The effect of CDs on the mechanical properties of PVA nanocomposite was studied. The tensile strength and elongation at break of PVA nanocomposite were increased by 113.01 % and 175.26 %, respectively. The shape memory and fluorescence properties of CDs/PVA nanocomposite were studied under different conditions such as water, acid, alkali and heat. The fluorescence intensity of CDs/PVA nanocomposites was enhanced due to the deprotonation of CDs surface under alkaline conditions. By coating CDs/PVA solution on LFP, a stable fluorescent fingerprint with clear ridge details was formed on the composite film, and it is applied to letterpress printing and gravure printing, enabling personal identification and ink-free printing. Obviously, the nanocomposite film prepared herein has multiple functions, providing potential for the design and manufacture of advanced all-round personal information identification and encryption applications in extremely harsh high temperature and strong alkaline environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin-derived carbon dots
Nanocomposite
Stimuli responsive
Fluorescent
Ink-free printing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and CDs/PVA nanocomposite film
Latent fingerprints identification and ink-free printing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Foundation of Zhejiang A & F University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Scientific Research Foundation of Zhejiang A and F University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project is supported by Scientific Research Foundation of Zhejiang A & F University ( 2022LFR102 ) and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LGN22C160003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Showing (a) Preparation of CDs from the lignin derivatives and process of LFP collection and recognition by means of CDs/PVA ink; (b) Illustration of the applications of CDs/PVA composites in ink-free printing.

(a) TEM images of CDs. Inset: high-resolution TEM image of CDs and diameter distribution histogram of CDs. (b) FTIR spectra of CDs. (c) Broad XPS spectra of CDs. (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s of CDs. (g) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (the inset photographs are under sunlight and under UV light). (h) FL spectra under different excitation wavelengths of CDs. (i) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs.

(a) Tensile stress-strain curves. (b) Tensile strength and elongation at break bar chart of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites. (c) FTIR spectra of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites. SEM images of surfaces (d) and cross sections (e) of PVA film. SEM images of surfaces (f) and cross sections (g) of CDs/PVA nanocomposites film. (h) Photograph of a flower underneath CDs/PVA film. (i) Light transmission of PVA and CDs/PVA film.
Shape recovery process of manually deformed CDs/PVA strips in (a) water (25.0 ℃), (b) alkali (25.0 ℃, pH = 11), (c) acid (25.0 ℃, pH = 2), (d) heat (70 ℃).

(a) Photograph of the flexible CDs/PVA film after collected LFP in glass sheets. (b) Image the flexible CDs/PVA film after collected LFP taken under irradiation with UV light (365 nm) (scale bar: 5 mm). (c, d) Laser confocal bright field microscopic images of a random zone of the LFP shown in (b) (scale bar: 500 µm). (e, f, g) microscopic detail features of the fingerprint (scale bar: 1 mm). (h) Photograph of CDs/PVA film collected fluorescent fingerprints under UV light after 60 days in air atmosphere.

(a) Relief printing process for Chinese character “Zhejiang A & F University” of printing in a clerical script style by using CDs/PVA nanocomposites as ink-free patterned substrates (scale bar: 1 cm). (b) Mechanism diagram of CDs flow within evaporating CDs/PVA solution covering the relief structure. (c) Physical photo of ginkgo leaf stone seal. (d) The fluorescence images of “Ginkgo biloba” and “1958″ patterns were developed on nano-composite film by ink-free printing technology. (e) The fluorescence image of “Ginkgo biloba” composite film was collected after shape memory processing in response to heat stimulation. (f) The fluorescence image of “1958″ composite film was collected after shape memory processing in response to alkaline stimulation.

head.ce:title: Latent fingerprints identification and ink-free printing of fluorescent lignin-derived CDs/PVA nanocomposite with multi-stimuli responsive shape memory behavior

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are the joint corresponding author to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development and comparison of latent fingerprint (LFP) are crucial for the detection and identification of LFP. However, research on stable, clear, and no background interference’s fluorescent materials for LFP recognition are scarce. In this study, lignin-derived carbon dots (CDs) with orange red emission were synthesized and combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix for identification of LFP and ink-free printing. The physicochemical and microstructure of the CDs and CDs/PVA nanocomposite have been precisely elucidated by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, SEM, and TGA to verify the reaction of CDs with PVA forming hydrogen bonds. The effect of CDs on the mechanical properties of PVA nanocomposite was studied. The tensile strength and elongation at break of PVA nanocomposite were increased by 113.01 % and 175.26 %, respectively. The shape memory and fluorescence properties of CDs/PVA nanocomposite were studied under different conditions such as water, acid, alkali and heat. The fluorescence intensity of CDs/PVA nanocomposites was enhanced due to the deprotonation of CDs surface under alkaline conditions. By coating CDs/PVA solution on LFP, a stable fluorescent fingerprint with clear ridge details was formed on the composite film, and it is applied to letterpress printing and gravure printing, enabling personal identification and ink-free printing. Obviously, the nanocomposite film prepared herein has multiple functions, providing potential for the design and manufacture of advanced all-round personal information identification and encryption applications in extremely harsh high temperature and strong alkaline environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent materials have broad applications in the fields of actuators, optoelectronics, photocatalysis and information technologies (Gao et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019a). It is ideal to enable luminescent materials to possess fluorescence, light harvest, and management properties, particularly when applied to latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection as labelling agents (Prabakaran and Pillay, 2020; Su et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Fingerprints detection is a powerful personal identification technique in forensic investigations since the uniqueness and lifelong invariance of fingerprints for individual (Dong et al., 2020; Jung et al., 2020). Due to personal fingerprint’s uniqueness and lifelong invariance, fingerprints detection exerts important effect in the field of forensic investigations (Liu et al., 2019a; Vo and De, 2016).

Fingerprints are the skin trace left on the touched object by the finger. Latent fingerprints on object require to be further processed before visualized by the naked eye (Shi et al., 2020). Fluorescence imaging, serving as a commonly conventional fingerprints detection method, employs the mode of adding photoluminescent or dyes nanomaterials on LFPs to displays high-contrast fluorescent signals under light irradiation (Li et al., 2020). Luminescent materials, including upconversion nanoparticles, quantum dots, and carbon dots (CDs), etc., have been utilized for the development of LFP detection (Fernando et al., 2015; Souza et al., 2016; Wen et al., 2018).

As a class of carbon-based nanomaterials, CDs show possess numerous outstanding merits, such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, unique dimmable photoluminescence, facile synthesis, and low cost (Li et al., 2023). Although several efforts suggest that CDs are of promising development prospect in the field of LFP detection, their widespread application is limited due to some shortcomings. First, traditional CDs display bright fluorescence in solution, and when CDs solutions are dried, the fluorescence of CDs is always quenched by aggregation effect (Huang et al., 2019; Yue et al., 2019). Secondly, the emission color of CDs is mostly blue or green, which can be interfered with by many substrates’ background fluorescence, especially under UV light (Yang et al., 2019a; Yang et al., 2019b). In addition, although some CD-based hybrid powders with weak fluorescence have been prepared recently, easily destructive fingerprint patterns and significant reduction of fluorescence from CDs-based fingerprint dusting are an ongoing problem. Therefore, exploring more effective way to prepared CDs for LFP identification remains an important scientific study (Li et al., 2018).

Lignin, as the main component of wood cells wall, is a rich natural aromatic polymer in nature (Chen et al., 2021). It is important to improve the utilization of lignin and its derivatives. At present, the research focusing on the preparation of CDs from lignin mainly includes lignin structure regulation, biosynthesis, and physical methods (Zhu et al., 2022). Lignin has renewable, sustainable, biocompatibility, and unique luminescent properties, making it an ideal precursor for preparing biomass illuminants (Tao et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021). In recent years, there were several reports to design and assemble lignin-based fluorescent materials. Herein, we developed a lignin-derived CDs materials with orange red emissive properties, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was selected as ink-free patterned polymer substrates, used for LFP recognition and printing by integrating the CDs with PVA ( Fig. 1) (El-Shamy and Zayied, 2020; Li et al., 2019b; Shen et al., 2022). The aim of this study is to prepare stable, clear, no background interference, and easily identifiable fluorescent fingerprint material and realize the efficient transfer, collection, and detection of LFP on substrates. The prepared fluorescent CDs can be well dispersed in PVA to form a transparent film as an ink-free substrate for the pattern. We evaluated the efficiency of LFP transfer and detection through feature point extraction and similarity analysis (Liu et al., 2019b). The bright orange red fluorescence fingerprint pattern formed on the CDs/PVA nanocomposite films are stable and clear without background fluorescence interference, which are convenient for personal identification, and realize high-efficient and non-destructive transfer, collection and detection of LFP on different substrates (Wang et al., 2021). As an application, this optical film was successfully used for relief printing and intaglio printing by an ink-free manner (Wang et al., 2018). Furthermore, thanks to the multi-stimuli responsive characteristics of CDs/PVA nanocomposites, the composite film can be used in extremely harsh pH and high temperature environments (Ma et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2019b).

Calcium lignosulfonate and o-phenylenediamine were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), ethanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with average Mw of 1750 ± 50 were provided by Macklin Co., Ltd, China. All reagents and solvents were used as received unless otherwise specified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and CDs/PVA nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: The 0.3 g calcium lignosulphonate, 0.9 g o-phenylenediamine were first mixed in 20 % H2SO4 and 20 % H3PO4 solution (30 mL) and stirred violently for 1 h at room temperature. Then, the above mixture was moved to a 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated for 10 h at 210 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, the as-prepared mixtures were centrifuged (8000 rpm, 15 min) to remove large particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was transferred to dialysis tubing (MWCO 1000 Da) in deionized water for 72 h. Finally, the purified CDs were dried at 70 °C to obtain solid materials for characterization purposes.

7 g PVA was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water, and magnetically stirred in an oil bath at 90 °C until PVA was completely dissolved. Then, 3 mL of CDs/ethanol solution (2 mg/mL) was added to the above PVA solution and stirred for 1 h until the CDs were evenly dispersed. The well dispersed mixture was centrifuged to eliminate bubbles and then was casted in a Teflon mold (approximately 16 cm × 22 cm) to obtain the CDs/PVA nanocomposite films at room temperature. For comparison purposes, PVA films without addition of CDs were prepared using the same method.

The morphologies of CDs were measured by a JEOL-JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet 6700 over the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was investigated by a smartlab 9X X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were evaluated using an Escalab 250 XI spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a UV-365 UV–VIS spectrometer. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs solution was determined by F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescent lifetime of CDs and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs/PVA nanocomposites were recorded using a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The thermal properties of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites were measured by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Q20) and differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, Q2000). The mechanical properties of the PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites were evaluated by tensile stress-strain measurements using an Electromechanical Universal Testing Machine (SANS, CMT6104, China) at room temperature. The sample for tensile measurements was a shape with 50 mm × 4 mm × 2 mm. The reported values of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites were the averages of six specimens. The results were statistically analyzed by analysis of variance (ANOVA). The morphology of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU-8230, Japan). The confocal fluorescence images were obtained using a Zeiss LSM 880 laser scanning confocal microscope.

The fluorescent CDs were fabricated from calcium lignosulphonate with o-phenylenediamine as a precursor by a one-step hydrothermal treatment method (Fig. 1a). TEM was used to characterize the morphologies of CDs. The CDs are observed to be evenly dispersed and the particles are approximately spherical in shape with an average diameter of 3.81 nm, as shown in Fig. 2a. The high-resolution TEM image confirms the high crystallinity with lattice spacing of 0.18 nm and a uniform size distribution, which is attributed to the (100) lattice planes of graphite framework (inset in Fig. 2a). The XRD pattern exhibits an obvious peak at 26.31°, corresponding to (002) crystal plane of the graphitization structure of CDs (Fig. S1). To further analyze the composition of CDs, FT-IR and XPS were also investigated. The FT-IR spectra of the CDs (Fig. 2b) present stretching vibrations of N/O-H groups at 3185 cm−1, C-H groups at 2360 cm−1, CC groups at 1618 cm−1, and C–O–C groups at 1125 cm−1. In addition, the peaks at 1320 and 761 cm−1 are associated with CN and N-H groups, respectively. The XPS spectra show three peaks at 284.08, 399.08, and 531.08 eV, indicating that the CDs consisted of C, N and O elements, and the calculated atomic ratios were 75.65 %, 12.48 % and 8.62 %, respectively (Fig. 2c). On the high-resolution XPS spectrum, C1s bands are separated into three peaks at 284.8, 285.58, and 288.08 eV, and assigned to C–C/CC, C–N and CO, respectively (Fig. 2d). The N1s bands exhibit two peaks at 399.58 and 400.68 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N groups (Fig. 2e). The O1s bands are fitted with two peaks at binding energy of 531.58 and 532.68 eV, assigned to C–O and CO, respectively (Fig. 2f). The above results of TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS spectroscopy demonstrate the carbonization of lignin derivatives into CDs with good surface functionalization.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-synthesized CDs displays strong absorption bands at 260–400 nm and 440–640 nm (Fig. 2g), corresponding to the π-π∗ transformation of the CC bands and n-π∗ transitions of the sp2 clusters of CO and CN bands (Lu et al., 2017), respectively, which demonstrates that CDs possess significant absorption in both the UV and visible light regions. Meanwhile, the CDs represent strong orange red fluorescence (FL) emission under UV light excitation as shown in the inset of Fig. 2g. To further explore the optical properties of CDs, we also obtained detailed fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 2h. At different excitation wavelength, all CDs have two emission peaks at 595 nm (orange light) and 645 nm (red light), and these emission peaks do not shift, indicating that CDs show excitation-independent fluorescence emission. FL lifetime of 2.71 ns is shown in the typical fluorescence decay curve of CDs (Fig. 2i). All above results manifest that CDs are of prominent, uniform, and unique orange red fluorescence emission characteristics.

The above work provided a comprehensive understanding of physicochemical structure and fluorescence properties of Lignin derived CDs. However, to achieve ideal applications, it is still necessary to overcome the inherent defect of fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of CDs in solution state (Song et al., 2014). According to previous reported work, encapsulating CDs into a polymer matrix is an effective method that can restrict the movement of CDs and make them uniformly dispersed. Using this strategy, CDs/PVA nanocomposites films were successfully prepared. The tensile stress-strain curves and tensile properties of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites are separately presented in Fig. 3a and b. Compared with neat PVA, CDs/PVA nanocomposites held the much higher strength of 25.3 MPa and elongation at break of 484.1 %, the ANOVA test showed that the increased were significant at the α = 0.001 level (P-value = 1.03 × 10−5) and α = 0.001 level (P-value = 4.2 × 10−6), respectively. The improvements in strength and elongation at break of CDs/PVA nanocomposites may be attribute to the formation of hydrogen bond inside the nanocomposite due to the addition of CDs (Li et al., 2019a). And compared with neat PVA, the tensile strength and elongation at break of CDs/PVA nanocomposite were increased by 113.01 % and 175.26 %, respectively. To further elucidate the interaction between components of nanocomposites, the FT-IR test was performed and FT-IR spectra of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 3c. Compared with PVA, the characteristic absorption peaks of the O–H and CO groups in CDs/PVA nanocomposites shifted from 3303 cm−1 and 1740 cm−1 to 3290 cm−1 and 1730 cm−1, respectively. The slight shifts indicate that hydrogen bond formation between CDs and PVA polymer chains, which alleviates the fluorescence quenching caused by CDs aggregation and enables CDs/PVA films to have excellent fluorescence behavior. The surface and cross-section morphology of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites were explored to identify the interaction of constituents. It could be clearly seen that CDs/PVA nanocomposites surface and cross-section are slightly rough compared with PVA (Fig. 3d–g). The results indicate that the hydrogen bond interactions from the micro-level inside the polymer structure, and improves the mechanical properties at the macro level. TGA was adopted to evaluate the thermal stability of PVA and CDs/PVA nanocomposites films in N2 atmosphere from 40 ℃ to 800 ℃. As shown in Fig. S2a and S2b, the first typical mass loss peak with a maximum degradation temperature of about 100 ℃ was due to evaporation of moisture in the samples. The remaining two degradation stages were ascribed to the degradation of PVA. The second maximum decomposition rate of CDs/PVA nanocomposites was lower than that of PVA (Fig. S2b). Also, the second maximum degradation temperature of PVA composite was a bit higher that of PVA of 322.8 ℃, indicating that the addition of CDs improves the thermal stability of polymer composites. The above results can be attributed to the presence of hydrogen bonds improved the physical crosslinking inside the polymer composites.

To further investigate the optical properties of the CDs/PVA nanocomposite film, the fluorescence spectroscopy test was performed at 540 nm excitation wavelength and the results were presented in Fig. S3. At 540 nm excitation wavelength, the CDs/PVA nanocomposite film showed two emission peaks located at around 595 nm (orange light) and 645 nm (red light). The fluorescence spectrum of the nanocomposite film was basically consistent with that of CDs solution. As shown in Fig. 3h, CDs/PVA film retains a high degree of transparency under visible lights. For quantitative analysis, the transmittances of PVA and CDs/PVA films were measured under UV–vis wavenumber from 200 to 800 nm). As displayed in Fig. 3i, the transmittance of CDs/PVA film was slightly lower than that of PVA in the low-wavenumber region (UV light). The reason is mainly due to the absorption action of CDs to UV light, as demonstrated in Fig. 2g. On the contrary, in high-wavenumber region (visible light), the transmittance of CDs/PVA film was significantly improved, reaching about 89.17 %, which was almost the same as that of PVA. These phenomena may be attributed to the uniform distribution of CDs in PVA polymer matrix.

As is known, hydrogen bonds act as molecular switches that can be widely regulated by changing heat, solvents, and pH, etc. In this regard, CDs/PVA nanocomposite films were cut into thin strips to check their shape recovery and fluorescence performance, which demonstrated their structural capabilities in this field (including hydrogen bonding based on FT-IR analysis). The shape memory behavior and fluorescence properties of CDs/PVA nanocomposite were measured by a classical bending test, including different stimuli such as water, pH, and heat. Firstly, the straight strips (original shapes) were put in oven of 80 °C (the heating temperature was set higher than the glass transition temperature (Tg) of PVA, and the Tg value was obtained from the DSC curve of Fig. S4) for 5 min, and then external force was applied to fix their temporary shapes as around 30°, keeping them at room temperature for 5 min. In the next step, the deformed strips were immersed into different pH solutions to evaluate the shape recovery performance. All shape changes were recorded by the camera, as shown in Fig. 4 and Video. S1-S4 (Supporting Information).

As shown in Fig. 4a, after soaking the deformed CDs/PVA strips in deionized water (pH=7) at ambient temperature (25 ℃) for about 120 s, the deformed strips began to become limp, due to the gradual partial swelling and dissolution of the deformed CDs/PVA samples. The fluorescence of the sample was gradually weak with the increasing of the reaction time in the neutral aqueous solution. However, the fluorescence of nanocomposites did not completely disappear. The effects of alkaline and acidic environments for deformed CDs/PVA strips are shown in Fig. 4b and c, respectively. For deformed strips exposed to alkaline environments, the best record for shape recovery was 150◦ in 200 s, and the fluorescence intensity of the composites also increased with the increasing impregnation time in the alkaline solution. Under acidic stimulation, the deformed strip ultimately reached a recovery angle of 175° in 150 s, and the fluorescence intensity of strip was gradually weakened during the recovery process. After returning to the original straight shape, the fluorescence of the strip gradually disappeared. Furthermore, the deformed CDs/PVA strips regained the angle of 170° within 6 s when it was rapidly immersed in heat atmosphere (70 ℃, Fig. 4d).

The pH-responsive fluorescence properties of CDs/PVA composites are attributed to the difference of ionization degree of the carboxyl group on the CDs surface in acidic and alkaline solutions, which transforms into protonated and deprotonated structures, resulting in changes in fluorescence characteristics. In acidic conditions, carboxyl protonation occurs on the surface of CDs (Tao et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019b). It’s facile for protonated carboxyl group absorb electrons, which reduces the electron density on the surface of CDs, forming some defects on the surface of CDs, thereby reducing the probability of electron excited transitions in CDs and exhibiting weaker fluorescence intensity. In alkaline conditions, the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs are deprotonated. Compared to carboxyl groups in the protonated state, the deprotonated carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs have more negative charges. On the other hand, deprotonation can weaken the ability to absorb electrons between carboxyl groups and carbon nuclei, increasing the conjugation degree of the entire CD system and reducing surface defects. Therefore, compared to acidic conditions, CDs/PVA nanocomposites exhibit higher fluorescence intensity under alkaline conditions. In addition, protonation/deprotonation of carboxyl groups on CDs surface can regulate the assembly/disassembly of hydrogen bonds between CDs and PVA, resulting in a lower shape recovery rate under alkaline conditions than that under acidic conditions. Therefore, greatly utilizing the different effect of shape recovery rate and fluorescence intensity of CDs/PVA nanocomposite, which is controlled by pH and temperature, can achieve its application in specific environments. The characteristics of CDs/PVA nanocomposite that adjusting pH and temperature could change shape recovery rate and fluorescence intensity, could be greatly utilized in specific environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent fingerprints identification and ink-free printing
ce:section.ce:para: It’ reported that CDs could be used as solid fluorescent powders or solutions for potential fingerprint enhancement. However, there are still issues with fluorescence quenching of CDs, unclear fluorescence color, and easy destruction of fingerprint patterns. In this study, the as-synthesized lignin-derived CDs had abundant chemical functional groups and fluorescence properties and could be well dispersed in the polymer matrix. The prepared CDs/PVA nanocomposite had excellent mechanical properties, thermal stability and pH responsive characteristics, which provides potential for LFPs collection and identification. The schematic illustration of the CDs/PVA nanocomposites for potential fingerprint collection and identification is shown in Fig. 1a. The CDs/PVA solution was added onto the glass sheets with latent fingerprint deposition. An independent flexible CDs/PVA transparent film formed after solvent evaporation and was easily peeled off from the glass sheets, leaving obvious ridges and groove details of the fingerprint ( Fig. 5a). Subsequently, by placing the obtained film under UV light, fingerprints can be observed clear ridge structures and bright orange red fluorescence (Fig. 5b). The fingerprint collected on CDs/PVA film was observed by laser confocal microscopy for microscopic morphology. We can see that the high-resolution fingerprints with ridge and furrow details, were well preserved on the film (Fig. 5c and d). which has the potential to be applied in the field of personal recognition. This is of great significance for identifying latent fingerprints with strong background interference problems and further analysis. To further verify fingerprint details, the latent fingerprint details collected by the CDs/PVA film was evaluated. In the enlarged images (Fig. 5e–g), arches, bifurcations and ridged terminations could be clearly seen by the naked eye, providing reliable evidence for individual identity recognition. In addition, the fluorescent fingerprints formed on the composite film were very stable, and the fluorescent color could still be observed after 60 days of irradiation with a UV light (Fig. 5h), which is attributed to the stable physicochemical properties of PVA effectively fixing CDs and their dispersed state in PVA matrix. Furthermore, the method was used to detect potential fingerprints left over from the past, it is found that the repeated collection of fingerprints will not damage the original potential fingerprint status.

To reveal the mechanism of potential fingerprint visualization and apply it to the field of printing. CDs/PVA nanocomposites were used as the ink-free pattern substrate, the process of printing the Chinese character “Zhejiang A & F University” in a clerical script style is similar to the process of fingerprint collection and recognition, as shown in Fig. 6a. The CDs/PVA nanocomposites solution was coated on the metal relief template; after the solvent was evaporated, the transparent film formed was peeled off from the metal template. The fluorescent words successfully displayed on the film, and brighter fluorescence was observed in the edge area of the words. During the solvent evaporation process, CDs migrated towards the relief part/fingerprint ridge edge due to the coffee ring effect caused by outward capillary flow (Fig. 6b). In addition, under the entropy driven order in the nanoparticle/polymer composite system, nanoparticles tend to migrate to the interface between the film and substrate. Therefore, when CDs/PVA films are formed on fingerprint substrate or relief templates, CDs will separate towards the interface between the CDs/PVA film and the patterned/embossed portion. Based on the visualization mechanism, we made “Ginkgo biloba” patterns and “1958″ numbers on CDs/PVA films (Fig. 6c and d). Similarly, the relief patterns on stone seal can also be effectively transferred and visualized through this ink-free printing technology.

Combined with the multi-stimuli responsive shape memory and fluorescence characteristics of CDs/PVA nanocomposites, we further verified the fluorescence changes of CDs/PVA films under different environmental conditions (heating, external force, acid and alkali solutions, etc.) after realizing ink-free printing. It found that the fluorescent patterns formed by CDs/PVA films are also not removed by heating, external force, and alkali solutions. In addition, CDs/PVA films with “Ginkgo biloba” patterns transferred from stone seal were folded and deformed under heat and then transferred to heat (around 100 ℃) (Fig. S5a). It was clearly observed that the fluorescence intensity of the surface patterns was not changed under UV light during the shape recovery of the films (Fig. 6e). Similarly, after the CDs/PVA film with “1958″ numbers were transferred from the stone seal under the action of heat was folded and deformed, then it was transferred to a strong alkaline solution (pH=12) (Fig. S5b–f). It could be clearly observed that during the shape recovery process of the film, the fluorescence intensity of the surface pattern under ultraviolet light is also enhanced (Fig. 6f). These results are consistent with the fluorescence changes of the CDs/PVA nanocomposite film strip during the shape recovery process in heat and alkali solution. The CDs/PVA nanocomposites prepared herein have multi-functionalities, providing a new perspective for design and manufacture of advanced all-round anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, and are expected to be applied in extremely harsh pH and high temperature environments.

In summary, orange red emission CDs prepared from abundant non-toxic lignin-derived materials were mixed with PVA to form a flexible film, providing a new green path for fingerprint recognition and ink-free printing. The physical crosslinking of CDs with PVA could significantly improve the mechanical properties of nanocomposite films due to the presence of hydrogen bonds. In addition, CDs/PVA nanocomposite films exhibit different fluorescence emission and excellent shape memory performance under different stimuli (water, acid, alkali, and heat). Encouragingly, thanks to the interface segregation mechanism of CDs in polymer films, this LFP morphology of nanocomposite films could be observed by UV light after being stored in the atmosphere for up to 60 days. This easily available and nontoxic nanocomposites film could be also applied to identify LFP that has been exposed for a long time. Importantly, this CDs/PVA nanocomposite films ink-free printing structure remains good fluorescence and morphology characteristics at high temperatures (100 ℃) and strong alkaline (pH=12) environments. Thereby, the high-performance CDs/PVA nanocomposites demonstrated in present work has good application prospects for personal information recognition and anti-counterfeiting encryption operated in extremely high temperature and/or strong alkaline environments.

Wenhao Yuan: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jie Zhou: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization. Mingyue Jiang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization. Qingfeng Sun: Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Gaoyuan Ye: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xiaochun Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Writing-original draft. Hongjie Bi: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by Scientific Research Foundation of Zhejiang A & F University (2022LFR102) and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LGN22C160003).
